practice.prompt: Why does inversion occur after "Never have I..."?
options: it is an irregular passive construction
a negative adverbial has been fronted, triggering auxiliary inversion
it is a cleft sentence pattern
practice.answer: a negative adverbial has been fronted, triggering auxiliary inversion

practice.prompt: Which sentence uses inversion correctly?
options: Not only he resigned, but he also published a statement.
Not only did he resign, but he also published a statement.
Not only did he resigned, but also he published a statement.
Not only he did resign, but he published also a statement.
practice.answer: Not only did he resign, but he also published a statement.

practice.prompt: Rarely [BLANK] this approach produce such consistent results. (does)
Only after the meeting [BLANK] the full implications become apparent. (did)
Construct the inversion:
practice.answer: Never have I seen such commitment

production.prompt: Rewrite these sentences using inversion to increase emphasis and formality: (1) "I have never witnessed such a rapid transformation." (2) "We only understood the risks after the incident occurred." (3) "The situation was so grave that all meetings were suspended."
production.hint: (1) Never have I... (2) Only after... did we... (3) So grave was the situation that...

production.prompt: Write a formal opening paragraph of 60-80 words for an essay on institutional accountability, incorporating at least two inverted structures. The opening should establish the argument with rhetorical force.
production.hint: Use "Never before has...", "Rarely do...", or "Only when..." to create a strong opening. Follow with normal word order to avoid monotony.

title: Cleft Sentences — It Was John Who / What I Need Is More Time
description: Cleft structures redistribute informational focus, placing emphasis on the element that matters most to the speaker.

explanation.text: CLEFT SENTENCES split a single clause into two, foregrounding a particular element. IT-CLEFT: "It was John who called." / "It is the methodology that needs revision." / "It was not until 1945 that the full scale was known." Structure: It + be + FOCUSED ELEMENT + relative clause. The focused element can be the subject, object, adverbial or time expression. WH-CLEFT (Pseudo-cleft): "What I need is more time." / "What surprised me most was the scale of the problem." / "What the data reveals is a pattern of systemic neglect." Structure: What + clause + be + FOCUSED ELEMENT. REVERSE WH-CLEFT: "The reason they failed is that they ignored the evidence." / "The thing that concerns me is the lack of transparency." ALL-CLEFT: "All I ask is that you consider the alternatives." / "All the evidence suggests is correlation, not causation." Clefts are primarily DISCOURSE tools — they signal: "this is the new or contrastive information."
explanation.note: IT-cleft focuses on what comes immediately after "it is/was": "It was the timing that mattered" (not the content). WH-cleft focuses on what comes AFTER the verb "be": "What mattered was the timing." Both foreground the same element but with slightly different information structure and textual rhythm.
explanation.tip: "All-cleft" is powerful but restrictive: it implies the focused element is the ONLY thing, creating a minimising or concessive effect: "All the data shows is a correlation." / "All I am suggesting is that we pause." Use it to de-escalate or to make a measured point in a debate.

examples.en: It was the lack of coordination, not the funding, that led to the project's failure.
What the research consistently demonstrates is the primacy of early intervention.
It was not until the second review that the fundamental flaw was identified.
What concerns me most is not the decision itself, but the process by which it was reached.
All I am arguing is that the current framework requires serious reconsideration.
The reason why this approach succeeds is that it aligns institutional incentives with social outcomes.

listening.text: It was the lack of coordination, not the funding, that led to the project's failure.
What the research consistently demonstrates is the primacy of early intervention.
It was not until the second review that the fundamental flaw was identified.
What concerns me most is not the decision itself, but the process by which it was reached.
All I am arguing is that the current framework requires serious reconsideration.
The reason why this approach succeeds is that it aligns institutional incentives with social outcomes.
What distinguishes a good argument from a weak one is the quality of its evidence.

repetition.text: It was the lack of coordination, not the funding, that led to the project's failure.
What the research consistently demonstrates is the primacy of early intervention.
What concerns me most is not the decision itself, but the process by which it was reached.
What distinguishes a good argument from a weak one is the quality of its evidence.

practice.prompt: What is the primary function of a cleft sentence?
options: to foreground a particular element and signal its informational importance
to avoid the use of relative clauses
practice.answer: to foreground a particular element and signal its informational importance

practice.prompt: Which correctly uses an it-cleft to emphasise "the methodology"?
options: It was the methodology which needs revision.
It is the methodology that needs revision.
What needs revision is that methodology it.
practice.answer: It is the methodology that needs revision.

practice.prompt: [BLANK] the evidence reveals is a pattern of institutional neglect. (Wh-cleft)
All I [BLANK] asking is that we consider the full range of options. (am)
Construct the it-cleft:
practice.answer: It was not the funding that the lack of coordination

production.prompt: Rewrite these sentences as clefts to shift the informational focus as indicated: (1) Emphasise "the evidence" → "The evidence changed the outcome." (2) Emphasise "the process" → "The process concerned me, not the outcome." (3) Minimise your claim → "I am only suggesting a delay."
production.hint: (1) It was the evidence that... (2) What concerned me was the process... (3) All I am suggesting is a delay.

production.prompt: Write a 70-word analytical paragraph about a topic of your choice. Use at least two cleft constructions to direct the reader's attention to your key claims.
production.hint: Use it-clefts for contrastive emphasis and wh-clefts to introduce your main analytical point.

title: Participle Clauses — Having Finished / Not Knowing / Born Into
description: Participle clauses condense information, increase textual density and signal C1 written sophistication.

explanation.text: PARTICIPLE CLAUSES reduce a full clause to a participial phrase, sharing the subject with the main clause. PRESENT PARTICIPLE (-ing): simultaneous or causal action. "Knowing the risks, she proceeded with caution." / "The committee, having considered all options, reached a unanimous decision." / "Lacking sufficient evidence, the prosecution could not proceed." PAST PARTICIPLE (V3): passive or completed state. "Born into poverty, he nevertheless reached the highest levels of academia." / "Exhausted by the negotiations, the delegates finally agreed." / "Seen from this perspective, the argument is considerably weaker." PERFECT PARTICIPLE (having + V3): action completed before the main clause. "Having reviewed the data, the team revised its initial conclusions." / "Having spent three years in the field, she brought unparalleled practical experience." NEGATIVE PARTICIPLE: "Not knowing what to expect, the delegation arrived cautiously." / "Not having been consulted, the committee expressed its frustration formally." DANGLING PARTICIPLE (error to avoid): The participle must share its subject with the main clause. WRONG: "Having reviewed the report, several errors were found." (the report did not review anything). CORRECT: "Having reviewed the report, the auditors found several errors."
explanation.note: Participle clauses are primarily a feature of formal written English. They increase density — one sentence does the work of two — and signal writer competence. In spoken C1 English, they appear in prepared speech (presentations, lectures) but are rare in spontaneous conversation.
explanation.tip: A quick test for dangling participles: ask "Who is doing the action in the -ing clause?" The answer must be the subject of the main clause. If it is not, the construction is dangling. This is one of the most common errors in academic writing at B2/C1 level.

examples.en: Having examined the available evidence, the researchers concluded that the initial hypothesis required revision.
Not knowing how to respond to the accusation, the spokesperson left the room without comment.
Driven by a commitment to transparency, the organisation published its full financial records.
Having been excluded from the preliminary discussions, the smaller nations raised formal objections.
Acknowledging the complexity of the issue, the panel declined to offer a definitive recommendation.
Born into a tradition of public service, she brought both a personal stake and a professional perspective to the debate.

listening.text: Having examined the available evidence, the researchers concluded that the initial hypothesis required revision.
Not knowing how to respond to the accusation, the spokesperson left the room without comment.
Driven by a commitment to transparency, the organisation published its full financial records.
Having been excluded from the preliminary discussions, the smaller nations raised formal objections.
Acknowledging the complexity of the issue, the panel declined to offer a definitive recommendation.
Born into a tradition of public service, she brought both a personal stake and a professional perspective.

repetition.text: Having examined the available evidence, the researchers concluded that the initial hypothesis required revision.
