options: hedges, throat-clearing openers, redundant qualifiers and inflated nominalisations
practice.answer: hedges, throat-clearing openers, redundant qualifiers and inflated nominalisations

practice.prompt: "It is arguably [BLANK] case that..." → revised: "Arguably..." (the)
"Due to the [BLANK] that..." → revised: "Because..." (fact)
Revise to remove inflated nominalisation:
practice.answer: The committee decided to reject the proposal

production.prompt: Revise the following passage applying all four levels: (1) macro: does the argument develop? (2) paragraph: is every sentence necessary? (3) sentence: are there vague modifiers or inflated nominalisations? (4) word: is the register consistent? "The issue of climate change is something that is very important and which has many implications. A lot of scientists have done research on it. Their findings are quite interesting and show that things need to change. It is important that we think about this carefully."
production.hint: (1) There is no argument — add one. (2) Cut orphan sentences. (3) Delete "very", "a lot of", "quite"; replace with precise vocabulary. (4) Raise register throughout.

production.prompt: Write a first draft of 100 words on any analytical topic, then revise it down to 70 words without losing any of the analytical content. Note what you cut and why. What did revision force you to see about your first draft?
production.hint: The 30% cut should come from: redundant hedges, vague modifiers, throat-clearing, repeated ideas, inflated nominalisations. What remains after cutting is your actual argument — leaner, clearer, stronger.

title: Extended Speaking — Sustaining Layered Argument
description: The ability to speak at length without strain, maintaining coherence across a multi-stage argument while adjusting in real time to audience response.

explanation.text: EXTENDED SPEAKING at C2 requires four simultaneous capacities: STRUCTURAL MAINTENANCE: holding the architecture of an argument in mind while speaking — knowing where you are, where you are going and how the current point connects to both. REAL-TIME ADJUSTMENT: sensing when a point has landed, when it needs development, when an objection is forming in the listener's mind and when to address it preemptively. REGISTER MODULATION: moving between more formal argument and more informal illustration or example without losing the thread — "Let me put that more concretely..." / "To give you a specific example of what I mean..." DISCOURSE MANAGEMENT UNDER PRESSURE: recovering when interrupted, managing multiple threads, returning to an abandoned point. C2 EXTENDED SPEAKING ARCHITECTURE: Opening commitment: "What I want to argue is that... and I'll make the case in three steps." Development: "The first of these is... [develop] ...which brings me to the second point..." Illustrative shift: "Let me be concrete for a moment..." Return to abstraction: "And this example illustrates the broader principle, which is..." Concession: "Now, the obvious objection to this is... and it deserves a serious answer." Rebuttal: "Even so, I would argue that..." Synthesis: "What these considerations together suggest is not [X] but [Y]..."
explanation.note: At C2, the most important single spoken competence is the ability to SLOW DOWN when the argument is complex and SPEED UP when making a point that should feel natural and obvious. The prosodic modulation of pace carries analytical meaning: slow = important/complex/controversial, fast = evidently true/illustrative/connective.
explanation.tip: "Let me step back for a moment." This phrase is one of the most valuable in extended speaking — it signals that you are about to reframe or zoom out, and it gives both speaker and listener a moment to orient. At C2, knowing when to step back is as important as knowing what to say next.

examples.en: What I want to suggest is this — and I'll make it in two steps, because the second depends entirely on the first.
Let me be concrete for a moment, because this is a point that tends to sound more abstract than it actually is in practice.
Now, the obvious objection here — and I want to take it seriously rather than dismiss it — is that the evidence is correlational, not causal.
Even granting that objection its full force, I think the broader argument still stands, for the following reason.
Let me step back for a moment and situate this point within the larger argument, because I think the connection is not immediately obvious.

What these three points together suggest — and this is where I want to come to rest — is not that X is false, but that the conditions under which it holds are far more limited than is typically assumed.

listening.text: What I want to suggest — and I'll make this in two steps, because the second depends on the first.
Let me be concrete for a moment — this tends to sound more abstract than it actually is in practice.
The obvious objection — and I want to take it seriously — is that the evidence is correlational, not causal.
Even granting that objection its full force, the broader argument still stands.
Let me step back and situate this within the larger argument — the connection is not immediately obvious.
What these three points together suggest is not that X is false, but that the conditions under which it holds are far more limited than assumed.
I want to hold two ideas in tension here, because I think the tension is the point — not a problem to be resolved.

repetition.text: I'll make this in two steps — the second depends entirely on the first.
Let me be concrete for a moment — this tends to sound more abstract than it actually is.
Even granting that objection its full force, the broader argument stands.
Not that X is false, but that the conditions under which it holds are far more limited than assumed.

practice.prompt: What does slowing down prosodically signal in extended academic speaking?
options: the speaker has forgotten the next point
the point is important, complex or potentially controversial — the listener should pay close attention
practice.answer: the point is important, complex or potentially controversial — the listener should pay close attention

options: the speaker is ending their contribution

the speaker disagrees with a previous point
practice.answer: a reframing or zoom-out that gives both speaker and listener a moment to orient

"Even [BLANK] that objection its full force, the argument still holds." (granting)

practice.answer: I want to argue this in two steps

production.prompt: Deliver (write as if spoken) a two-minute extended argument (250-300 words) on the following: "The way we measure economic success is fundamentally inadequate." Use: an opening commitment, a two-stage development, a concrete illustration, an anticipated objection with rebuttal, and a synthesising close.
production.hint: "What I want to argue is... The first reason is... [develop]. Let me put that concretely... This illustrates the broader principle... Now, the obvious objection is... Even granting this, however... What these points together suggest is..."

production.prompt: Write a 200-word spoken response to an interruption: you are in the middle of developing a complex argument when someone raises a different point. First acknowledge the interruption, hold your thread, address the interruption briefly, then return to your argument and complete it.
production.hint: "That's an important point — I'll come back to it. Before I do, let me finish the thread I was following, because the point you've raised actually connects to where I was heading... [complete argument]... And this brings me, in fact, to your point, which I want to address seriously."

title: Listening to Dense Input — Implication, Attitude and Argument Structure
description: C2 listening is not passive reception — it is active analysis of what is meant, how it is said, what is withheld, and how the argument is structured.

explanation.text: C2 LISTENING requires four analytical layers simultaneously: (1) CONTENT: what is being said — the propositional content of the utterance. (2) STANCE: how the speaker feels about it — certainty, reservation, irony, enthusiasm, scepticism. This is carried by: hedging vocabulary ("it seems", "I'd argue"), intonation (fall-rise for reservation, fall for commitment), modal choices (must/might/could), evaluative lexis (troubling, reassuring, striking). (3) IMPLICATION: what is meant but not said — scalar implicature, violation of maxims, strategic omission. (4) STRUCTURE: how the argument is organised — where is the thesis? Where is the concession? Where is the pivot? LISTENING FOR ATTITUDE: "It's an interesting approach" — is this sincere or damning with faint praise? The fall-rise on "interesting" signals reservation. "The evidence is... suggestive." — the hesitation before "suggestive" and the fall-rise signal that the speaker is understating a problem with the evidence. "As some have argued..." — the distancing device signals the speaker does not personally endorse the claim. FOLLOWING LONG ARGUMENTS: create a mental map as you listen. Identify: the opening thesis, the first supporting point, the concession, the rebuttal. If you lose the thread, the question "So your central claim is X — is that right?" is a legitimate C2 recovery move that also demonstrates analytical listening.
explanation.note: The most important C2 listening skill is the ability to track speaker STANCE — not just content. Two speakers can say the same thing ("The evidence is consistent") and mean very different things by it. The difference is in the prosody, the hedging and the lexical choices. Listening only for content misses the most important layer of spoken discourse.
explanation.tip: "So if I understand you correctly, your central claim is X, and your strongest piece of evidence is Y — is that a fair summary?" This comprehension check at C2 is not a question about the facts — it is a demonstration that you have understood the argumentative structure, not just the content. It also opens space for the speaker to refine or clarify.

examples.en: "It's a compelling argument." ↘↗ — reservation signalled by fall-rise. "It's a compelling argument." ↘ — genuine endorsement signalled by fall.
"As some have argued..." — the distancing device signals the speaker does not personally endorse the claim that follows.
"The evidence is... consistent." — the hesitation before "consistent" signals the speaker considers this a weaker claim than it sounds.
"Needless to say, the implications are significant." — the implicature: the implications may not be obvious; this is drawing attention to them.
"That's certainly one way of reading it." — implicature: I read it differently, and my reading is more defensible.
"So if I understand you correctly, your core claim is X and your strongest support is Y — is that a fair reading?"

listening.text: "It's a compelling argument." — listen for fall-rise (reservation) vs full fall (endorsement).
"As some have argued..." — distancing: the speaker does not personally endorse what follows.
"The evidence is... consistent." — hesitation signals the speaker is softening a concern.
"Needless to say, the implications are significant." — implicature: drawing attention to something the listener may not have noticed.
"That's certainly one way of reading it." — implicature: I read it differently.
"So your core claim is X and your strongest support is Y — is that fair?" — analytical comprehension check.
"I wouldn't go quite that far — but the direction is right." — speaker is partially endorsing, partially reserving.

repetition.text: "It's a compelling argument." — with fall-rise: reservation. With full fall: endorsement. Same words; different meanings.
"As some have argued..." — distancing device. The speaker is attributing, not endorsing.
"That's certainly one way of reading it." — polished way of saying: I see it differently.
"I wouldn't go quite that far — but the direction is right." — partial endorsement with precision.

practice.prompt: What is the most important layer of C2 listening beyond content?
options: speaker stance — certainty, reservation, irony, scepticism — carried by prosody, hedging and lexical choice
practice.answer: speaker stance — certainty, reservation, irony, scepticism — carried by prosody, hedging and lexical choice

practice.prompt: "As some have argued..." signals:
options: the speaker strongly endorses the claim
the speaker is attributing the claim to others and distancing themselves from it
the speaker has forgotten the source
practice.answer: the speaker is attributing the claim to others and distancing themselves from it

practice.prompt: "That's certainly one [BLANK] of reading it." — implicature: I see it differently. (way)
"I wouldn't [BLANK] quite that far — but the direction is right." (go)
Construct the analytical comprehension check:
practice.answer: So your core claim is X is that fair

production.prompt: Write five listening analysis notes — one sentence each — for what each of the following utterances means at the level of implication and stance (not content): (1) "That's an interesting take." (2) "The evidence is... promising." (3) "As others have suggested..." (4) "I wouldn't entirely disagree with that." (5) "It's certainly worth considering."
production.hint: For each: what is the literal content? What does the hesitation, hedging or distancing signal? What is the speaker actually communicating? How does the prosodic context change the meaning?

production.prompt: Write a 150-word spoken summary of an imagined complex academic lecture you have just heard. Demonstrate C2 analytical listening by: naming the central claim, identifying the main support, noting the concession, identifying the weakest point, and asking one analytical question that shows you understood the argument structure rather than just the content.
production.hint: "The central claim, as I understood it, was... The main evidence offered was... The speaker did acknowledge that... I found less convincing the step from X to Y... My question would be: does the argument hold if [condition changes]?"

title: Debate and Persuasion C2 — Tonal Control and Strategic Disagreement
description: C2 debate is not about winning — it is about the precise management of tone, disagreement, concession and reframing to advance a position without alienating the audience.

explanation.text: C2 DEBATE COMPETENCE rests on five skills: TONAL CONTROL: varying between authoritative assertion, careful hedging, collegial concession and pointed challenge — and knowing which is appropriate when. STRATEGIC DISAGREEMENT: disagreeing in a way that advances the argument rather than merely opposing: "I take the point entirely — and I'd go further: even if X is true, Y still holds." REFRAMING: changing the terms of the debate: "The question isn't whether X — the question is what X means for Y." STRATEGIC CONCESSION: conceding minor points to preserve the integrity of the major claim: "I'll grant that the evidence here is not definitive — but definitive evidence is not what the argument requires." RHETORICAL TIMING: knowing when a short direct sentence is more persuasive than a qualified one; when to pause; when to change pace; when an ironic aside will land and when it will alienate. C2 DEBATE PHRASES — DISAGREEMENT WITH PRECISION: "I'd push back on that quite firmly, actually." / "That's a fair summary of one reading — but I think it misses the central issue." / "With respect, I don't think that follows." / "I'd want to challenge the framing itself." CONCESSION: "That's a fair point — I'll take it. It doesn't, however, affect the core claim." / "I concede the evidence is mixed — but the direction is clear." REFRAMING: "We seem to be talking past each other — let me try to locate the actual point of disagreement."
explanation.note: "With respect" in English debate is a classical face-softening device — it signals that a pointed challenge is coming while acknowledging the challenger's status. But it can sound formulaic. "I'd push back on that quite firmly, actually" is more direct and often more effective because the word "actually" signals genuine conviction rather than conventional courtesy.
explanation.tip: "Let me try to locate the actual point of disagreement." — this reframing move is one of the most powerful in debate. It suggests that the apparent disagreement may be partly definitional or structural, and positions the speaker as analytically sophisticated rather than merely oppositional. It shifts the debate from confrontation to joint analysis.

examples.en: I take your point about the evidence — and I'd go further: even if that critique holds, it doesn't affect the structural argument.
With respect, I don't think that follows — the move from the empirical observation to the normative conclusion requires an additional premise that hasn't been supplied.
I'll grant that the evidence here is not conclusive. But conclusive evidence is precisely what the argument does not require.
We seem to be talking past each other — let me try to identify where the real disagreement lies.
That's a fair summary of the mainstream position. My claim is that the mainstream position is wrong, and here's why.
I'd challenge the framing itself — the question isn't whether X, but whether X implies Z, and on that the evidence is quite different.

listening.text: I take your point — and I'd go further: even if that critique holds, it doesn't affect the structural argument.
With respect, that move — from empirical observation to normative conclusion — requires a premise that hasn't been supplied.
I'll grant the evidence is not conclusive. But conclusive evidence is not what the argument requires.
We seem to be talking past each other — let me try to identify where the real disagreement lies.
That's a fair summary of the mainstream position. My claim is that the mainstream position is wrong.
I'd challenge the framing itself — the question isn't whether X, but whether X implies Z.
That's a serious objection. I want to address it head-on rather than sidestep it — because if I can't answer it, I shouldn't be making this argument.

repetition.text: I take your point — and I'd go further: even if that holds, it doesn't affect the structural argument.
I'll grant the evidence is not conclusive. But conclusive evidence is not what the argument requires.
We seem to be talking past each other — let me identify where the real disagreement lies.
The question isn't whether X — it's whether X implies Z. On that, the evidence is different.

practice.answer: conceding minor points to preserve the integrity of the major claim

practice.answer: We're talking past each other let me locate the disagreement

production.prompt: Write a 200-word debate response to the following position: "Immigration primarily benefits receiving countries, not sending ones." Your response must include: genuine engagement with a strong version of the claim, a strategic concession of a minor point, a reframing of the central question, a pointed challenge to one key premise, and a synthesising conclusion that advances rather than merely opposes.
production.hint: "The claim has considerable merit in certain respects — I'll grant that... However, the question is not whether... but whether... The premise that [X] is precisely what requires examination... Even if [conceded point], the structural argument is that... What the full picture suggests is..."

production.prompt: Write a 150-word debate opening statement on any contested topic you know well. The opening must: commit to a thesis, signal awareness of the main objection, indicate how the argument will address it, and close with a sentence that creates anticipation for what follows.
production.hint: "What I want to argue today is [thesis]. The obvious challenge to this position is [objection], and I will address it directly. The case rests on three considerations... But I want to begin with what I think is the question the debate has not yet adequately framed."

title: Improvisation and Reformulation — Real-Time Precision
description: The most demanding C2 spoken skill: the ability to find the right formulation under time pressure, to reformulate when a first attempt falls short, and to maintain precision without the support of preparation.

explanation.text: IMPROVISATION at C2 is not speaking without thinking — it is the ability to think in English at the same time as speaking, without the performance degrading to a lower level under pressure. This requires: LEXICAL ACCESS SPEED: not hesitating because the precise word is unavailable — either having it or having an immediate workaround: "I'm looking for the word... something like the gap between what is intended and what is achieved — the implementation gap, if you will." REFORMULATION STRATEGIES: first-attempt correction: "That is — or rather — what I mean is..." / "Let me put that differently." / "What I'm trying to say is..." / "Or, to be more precise..." REAL-TIME QUALIFICATION: adding hedges, scope limits and qualifications as you speak, not after: "This tends to be the case — at least in the institutional contexts I know best — with perhaps some variation in..." APPROXIMATION WITH FLAGGING: "something in the vicinity of X" / "roughly speaking" / "in the ballpark of" / "what amounts to" / "what I would loosely describe as" — these phrases allow you to continue while signalling that you are being approximate. RECOVERY FROM COMPLEXITY: "Let me start again, because I think I got tangled in the subordinate clauses. The main point is simply this: [restate]."
explanation.note: "If you will" — added after a formulation — signals that the speaker is using a term with some self-awareness, often because it is slightly informal, approximate or technical in a way that deserves acknowledgement: "The implementation gap, if you will." It is a sophisticated spoken hedge that maintains fluency while flagging the imprecision.
explanation.tip: "The main point is simply this:" — this phrase, used after a complex sentence that has become over-entangled, is one of the most elegant recovery moves in spoken English. It signals that the speaker recognises the preceding complexity and is about to cut through it. Far better than trailing off or repeating the tangle.

examples.en: What I'm getting at — or trying to, rather — is the distinction between how the system is designed and how it actually operates under pressure.
Let me reformulate that more precisely, because I don't think I captured it quite right: what I mean is not X per se but the conditions that make X possible.
There's a word for this — the gap between formal rules and actual behaviour — institutional hypocrisy, if you will, though that's perhaps stronger than I intend.
The main point is simply this: the argument works, but only under conditions that the evidence suggests are rarely met.
Something in the vicinity of cognitive dissonance — though the phenomenon I'm describing is perhaps more structural than psychological.
I got tangled there — let me try again. The core claim is that [plain restatement].

listening.text: What I'm getting at — or trying to — is the distinction between how the system is designed and how it actually operates under pressure.
Let me reformulate: what I mean is not X per se, but the conditions that make X possible.
The gap between formal rules and actual behaviour — institutional hypocrisy, if you will, though perhaps stronger than I intend.
The main point is simply this: the argument works, but only under conditions the evidence suggests are rarely met.
Something in the vicinity of cognitive dissonance — though more structural than psychological.
I got tangled — let me try again. The core claim is [plain restatement].
Or rather — and this is more precise — what I'm describing is not the failure itself but the conditions that made it inevitable.

repetition.text: Or rather — what I mean is not X per se, but the conditions that make X possible.
The main point is simply this: the argument works, but only under conditions rarely met.
Something in the vicinity of cognitive dissonance — though more structural than psychological.
I got tangled — let me try again. The core claim is simply this.

practice.prompt: "If you will" after a formulation signals:
options: the speaker is using a term self-consciously — acknowledging it is approximate, informal or deserving of qualification
the speaker is asking for confirmation
practice.answer: the speaker is using a term self-consciously — acknowledging it is approximate, informal or deserving of qualification

practice.prompt: "The main point is simply this:" is used to:
options: cut through a tangled sentence and restate the core claim clearly
practice.answer: cut through a tangled sentence and restate the core claim clearly

practice.prompt: "Let me [BLANK] that more precisely — what I mean is not X but the conditions that make X possible." (reformulate)
"[BLANK] rather — what I'm describing is not the failure but the conditions that made it inevitable." (Or)
Construct the recovery move:
practice.answer: The main point is simply this

production.prompt: Write a 200-word improvised spoken explanation (as you would actually say it, with real-time reformulation) of a complex idea you know well — a concept from your field, a historical process, a philosophical distinction. Include: at least two reformulation moves ("or rather", "let me put that differently"), one approximation with flagging ("something in the vicinity of"), one recovery from a tangled sentence, and one moment of deliberate simplification ("the main point is simply this:").
production.hint: Do not aim for polished prose — aim for spoken authenticity. The reformulations and recoveries should be genuine improvements, not mere decoration. The result should read as real-time thought.

production.prompt: You are in the middle of explaining a complex point when you realise your formulation was imprecise. Write the first (imprecise) attempt and then the reformulation that captures it more accurately. Do this three times with three different kinds of imprecision: (1) too broad, (2) wrong register, (3) missing a crucial qualification.
production.hint: (1) "[Broad statement] — or rather, to be more specific, what I mean is..." (2) "[Colloquial version] — let me put that more precisely..." (3) "[Unqualified claim] — though I should add, this only holds when..."

title: Academic and Intellectual Discourse C2
description: Operating as a full participant in English-medium academic and intellectual life: conferences, seminars, publications and scholarly debate.

explanation.text: C2 ACADEMIC DISCOURSE means participating in intellectual life in English as a full, not merely competent, contributor. This requires: CONFERENCE PARTICIPATION: presenting, asking questions, responding to questions under pressure, engaging in corridor conversations that develop into serious intellectual exchanges. SCHOLARLY WRITING CONVENTIONS: abstract writing (150 words that do the full argumentative work of the paper), literature review synthesis, methodology justification, discussion section that distinguishes findings from implications. PEER REVIEW LANGUAGE: "The central claim is insufficiently defended...", "The methodology, while rigorous, does not fully address...", "The implications drawn exceed what the data can sustain...", "The contribution would be strengthened by...", "The framing in Section 2 creates an unresolved tension with...". ACADEMIC CONVERSATION CONVENTIONS: "That's a fascinating question — let me think about it for a moment." / "I'm not sure the paper fully addresses that, actually — it's something I've been grappling with." / "The tension you're identifying is one I've noticed as well, and I don't think it's resolved." RESPONDING TO CRITICISM: "That's a serious critique — I want to take it seriously." / "I think you've identified a genuine weakness in the argument." / "I'd push back on that reading, though I take the broader point."
explanation.note: Responding to academic criticism under pressure is a specific C2 competence. The three unacceptable responses are: defensive dismissal, total capitulation, and deflection. The C2 response acknowledges the critique specifically ("You've identified a tension in Section 3 between X and Y that I don't fully resolve"), evaluates it honestly ("I think this is a genuine weakness"), and either defends or concedes with reasoning.
explanation.tip: "I've been grappling with that." — this phrase signals genuine intellectual engagement at the highest level. It says: I know this problem, I have not solved it, and I am taking it seriously. It creates intellectual credibility precisely because it does not pretend to have all the answers — which is the mark of genuine scholarship.

examples.en: The paper makes a strong empirical contribution; where it is less convincing is in the move from the findings to the broader theoretical implications.
You've identified a tension that I haven't fully resolved — and I think you're right that it matters for the argument.
The methodology is sound, but the discussion section draws implications that the data cannot fully sustain.
I want to take that critique seriously rather than deflect it: here is how I think the argument can be defended, and here is where I think it genuinely needs strengthening.
That's a question I've been grappling with — and I don't have a clean answer, which may itself be a finding.
The framing in Section 2 creates a tension with the conclusion that I noticed while writing it and haven't yet resolved to my own satisfaction.

listening.text: Strong empirical contribution — less convincing in the move from findings to broader theoretical implications.
You've identified a tension I haven't fully resolved — and you're right that it matters for the argument.
The methodology is sound, but the discussion draws implications the data cannot fully sustain.
I want to take that critique seriously: here is my defence, and here is where the argument genuinely needs strengthening.
A question I've been grappling with — and I don't have a clean answer, which may itself be a finding.
A tension I noticed while writing it and haven't resolved to my own satisfaction.
The contribution is significant precisely because it raises questions it cannot fully answer — that is what opens a field.

repetition.text: Strong contribution — less convincing in the move from empirical findings to theoretical implications.
You've identified a tension I haven't resolved — and you're right that it matters.
I want to take that critique seriously, not deflect it.
A question I've been grappling with — I don't have a clean answer, which may itself be a finding.

practice.prompt: The three unacceptable responses to academic criticism are:
options: defensive dismissal, total capitulation and deflection
practice.answer: defensive dismissal, total capitulation and deflection

practice.prompt: "I've been grappling with that." signals:
options: genuine intellectual engagement — the speaker knows the problem and is taking it seriously without pretending to have solved it
an attempt to change the subject
practice.answer: genuine intellectual engagement — the speaker knows the problem and is taking it seriously without pretending to have solved it

practice.prompt: "The discussion draws implications the data cannot fully [BLANK]." (sustain)
"The [BLANK] is significant because it raises questions it cannot fully answer." (contribution)
Construct the honest academic response:
practice.answer: You've identified a tension I haven't resolved

production.prompt: Write a 200-word response to a conference question that has identified a genuine weakness in your argument. The response must: name the weakness specifically, evaluate it honestly (neither dismiss nor fully capitulate), defend the aspects of the argument that survive the critique, acknowledge what would need to be changed to address it fully, and end with an intellectual opening rather than a defensive close.
production.hint: "You've identified a tension in the argument between [X] and [Y] — and I think you're right that this matters. Let me try to address it directly rather than sidestep it... The core claim survives because... What would need to change is... And I think the question you've raised opens rather than closes the issue."

production.prompt: Write a 150-word peer review comment on a hypothetical paper on a topic you know well. The comment must: identify a specific strength, identify a specific weakness, make one suggestion for development, and frame all of this in standard peer review language.
production.hint: "The paper makes a strong contribution to [field] by [specific claim]. The methodology is [evaluation]. The central weakness lies in the move from [X] to [Y], where the argument would benefit from [specific development]. The contribution would be strengthened by [concrete suggestion]."

title: Professional English C2 — High-Stakes Communication
description: C2 professional English operates in situations where precision, register and strategic communication have real consequences: negotiations, presentations, crisis communications and complex stakeholder management.

explanation.text: C2 PROFESSIONAL ENGLISH in high-stakes contexts requires: NEGOTIATION LANGUAGE: "We would be prepared to consider X, provided that Y is addressed." / "That is outside what we can agree to in this context — though I'm willing to explore whether there is a formulation that addresses your concern." / "I want to be transparent about our position: we cannot move on X, but we have flexibility on Y and Z." PRESENTATION UNDER PRESSURE: handling difficult questions ("That's a legitimate concern — let me address it directly, rather than defer it to a Q&A."), managing hostile audiences ("I appreciate that this is a controversial position — the evidence for it is, I believe, nonetheless substantial."), signalling confidence without false certainty. CRISIS COMMUNICATION: acknowledging without over-committing ("The situation is serious, and we take it seriously. We are not yet in a position to characterise it fully, but we will do so as soon as we can."), distinguishing between what is known and what is being investigated, maintaining credibility under information pressure. HIGH-STAKES EMAIL: subject line precision, opening that signals the email's purpose and register immediately, body that manages information hierarchy (most important first), close that specifies next steps unambiguously without sounding aggressive.
explanation.note: In high-stakes professional communication, the most dangerous mistake is saying more than you know. "I am not yet in a position to..." and "We will clarify this once we have the full picture" protect credibility far better than premature certainty that later proves wrong. C2 professional competence includes knowing when to slow down the communication.
explanation.tip: "I want to be transparent about our position." — this phrase, used at the opening of a negotiation or difficult communication, creates immediate credibility. It signals: I am not playing games; here is where I actually stand. In high-stakes contexts, this kind of honesty is strategically more powerful than positioning.

examples.en: We would be prepared to consider that arrangement, provided that the timeline question is addressed to our satisfaction first.
I want to be transparent about our position: we cannot move on the core terms, but we have genuine flexibility on the implementation structure.
That is a legitimate challenge — I would rather address it directly now than defer it to a Q&A where I will be asked the same question under greater pressure.
We are not yet in a position to characterise the full extent of the situation, but we take it seriously and will provide a complete update within 48 hours.
The evidence for this position is, I believe, substantial — I acknowledge that it is contested, and I welcome that challenge rather than avoid it.
I appreciate that this is not the answer you were hoping for. It is, however, the accurate one — and I think that matters more in the long run.

listening.text: We would consider that arrangement, provided the timeline question is addressed to our satisfaction first.
I want to be transparent: we cannot move on the core terms, but we have genuine flexibility on implementation.
I'd rather address that challenge directly now than defer it to a Q&A where the pressure is greater.
We're not yet in a position to characterise the full situation — we'll provide a complete update within 48 hours.
The evidence is substantial — contested, I acknowledge — and I welcome that challenge.
Not the answer you were hoping for. It is, however, the accurate one — and accuracy matters more in the long run.
Our position is clear: we are prepared to move on three of the five points; the remaining two are not negotiable at this stage.

repetition.text: I want to be transparent: we cannot move on the core terms, but we have flexibility on implementation.
I'd rather address that challenge directly now than defer it.
Not yet in a position to characterise the full situation — update within 48 hours.
Not the answer you were hoping for — but the accurate one, which matters more in the long run.

practice.prompt: In high-stakes professional communication, saying more than you know:
options: damages long-term credibility when premature certainty proves wrong
is always appropriate when stakeholders are anxious
practice.answer: damages long-term credibility when premature certainty proves wrong

practice.prompt: "I want to be transparent about our position." achieves what strategic purpose?
options: signals weakness and willingness to compromise
creates immediate credibility by signalling honest direct dealing rather than positional games
practice.answer: creates immediate credibility by signalling honest direct dealing rather than positional games

practice.prompt: "We would be [BLANK] to consider that arrangement, provided Y is addressed." (prepared)
"We are not [BLANK] in a position to characterise the full situation." (yet)
Construct the transparent negotiation opener:
practice.answer: I want to be transparent about our position

production.prompt: Write a 200-word high-stakes email to a senior stakeholder following a project setback. The email must: acknowledge the setback without over-committing to causes, distinguish between what is known and what is being investigated, maintain credibility by being precise about uncertainty, specify next steps clearly, and close in a way that is neither falsely reassuring nor alarmist.
production.hint: "I am writing to update you on [X], which has developed in ways we did not anticipate. The immediate situation is [factual]. The causes are [what we know vs what is being investigated]. We are not yet in a position to [X], but we will be by [specific date]. Our immediate next steps are [specific]. I am available to discuss this at your earliest convenience."

production.prompt: Write a 150-word presentation opening that addresses a hostile or sceptical audience on a controversial topic. The opening must: acknowledge the controversy directly, signal the strength of the evidence, invite challenge rather than avoid it, and commit to a specific argumentative structure.
production.hint: "I am aware that this position is contested — and I think that's appropriate, given what's at stake. What I want to argue is [thesis], and I want to make the case as directly as I can. The evidence for this position is substantial, though I acknowledge it is not without dispute. I will address the strongest objections directly, because if I cannot answer them, I should not be making this argument."

title: Literary and Cultural Reading — Style, Tone and Subtlety
description: Reading literary and culturally dense texts with the analytical and aesthetic engagement that C2 requires: attending to style, tone, implication and the gap between what is said and what is meant.

explanation.text: C2 LITERARY READING means engaging with texts not just for content but for how meaning is made — through style, voice, irony, selection, omission and the specific choices of a writer who controls language with intention. ATTENDING TO STYLE: word choice (the difference between "walked" and "strode" and "shuffled"), sentence rhythm (short sentences for intensity, long ones for accumulation), figurative language (metaphors that illuminate vs metaphors that obscure), and the texture of a prose that accumulates meaning across paragraphs. TONE: the implied attitude of the author toward the subject and the reader — detached, ironic, compassionate, satirical, wistful, impassioned. Tone is constructed by lexical choice, sentence structure, what is said and what is conspicuously not said. CULTURAL DENSITY: texts from different periods and traditions carry assumptions, allusions and cultural references that require background knowledge. A C2 reader can identify where a text is culturally dense, locate the relevant context, and read through it. LITERARY CRITICISM LANGUAGE: "The prose enacts rather than merely describes...", "The irony is structural, not incidental...", "The ambiguity is deliberate — the text resists resolution...", "The choice of [word] is loaded with...", "The narrative voice performs a kind of...".
explanation.note: "The text refuses easy interpretation." — this phrase signals C2 literary awareness. Some texts resist resolution precisely because their meaning is located in the tension between competing readings. A C2 reader does not try to "solve" an ambiguous text — they hold the ambiguity and describe what it does.
explanation.tip: "The prose enacts what it describes." This is one of the most powerful observations in literary analysis. It means: the style itself performs the meaning — not just reports it. A description of fragmentation that is itself fragmented. A sentence about excess that is itself excessive. Attending to this dimension separates C2 literary analysis from B2 content summary.

examples.en: The prose does not merely describe anxiety — it enacts it: the sentence structures elongate, qualify and double back on themselves in a way that formally mirrors the psychological state being depicted.

The irony here is structural rather than local: it is not a matter of individual phrases but of the entire framing of the narrative, which invites one reading on the surface and systematically undermines it beneath.
The ambiguity is deliberate — the text makes available two incompatible readings and refuses to adjudicate between them.

The choice of 'strode' rather than 'walked' or 'moved' is not incidental: it carries a freight of confidence, purpose and social authority that the narrative is simultaneously performing and ironising.
What the text conspicuously does not say — the absence at the centre of the narrative — turns out to be as significant as anything that is explicitly present.
The narrative voice maintains an ironic distance throughout that allows it to be simultaneously deeply engaged with its subject and critically detached from it.

listening.text: The prose enacts anxiety: sentences elongate, qualify and double back in ways that formally mirror the psychological state depicted.
The irony is structural, not local — it inhabits the entire framing of the narrative, not individual phrases.
The ambiguity is deliberate: two incompatible readings, and the text refuses to adjudicate between them.
'Strode' rather than 'walked' — carrying confidence, purpose and social authority the narrative simultaneously performs and ironises.
What the text does not say — the absence at the centre — is as significant as what is explicitly present.
Simultaneously engaged with its subject and critically detached from it — that is the signature of the ironic voice.
The text refuses easy interpretation — and that resistance is not a flaw but the point.

repetition.text: The prose enacts rather than merely describes — the sentence structure formally mirrors the psychological state.
Structural irony: not individual phrases but the entire framing that invites one reading and undermines it.
The absence at the centre — what the text does not say — is as significant as what it does.
The text refuses easy interpretation. That resistance is not a flaw — it is the point.

practice.prompt: "The prose enacts what it describes" means:
options: the style itself performs the meaning — the form formally mirrors the content
the text is easy to understand
the writer is repeating information unnecessarily
practice.answer: the style itself performs the meaning — the form formally mirrors the content

practice.prompt: A "deliberate ambiguity" in literary analysis signals:
options: a text that makes available two incompatible readings and refuses to resolve the tension between them
a metaphor that is difficult to understand
practice.answer: a text that makes available two incompatible readings and refuses to resolve the tension between them

practice.prompt: "The irony is [BLANK], not local — it inhabits the entire framing." (structural)
"The text [BLANK] easy interpretation — and that resistance is the point." (refuses)

practice.answer: The prose enacts rather than merely describes

production.prompt: Write a 150-word literary analysis of the following passage, attending to style, tone, word choice and what is conspicuously not said: "He walked into the room. Everyone looked up. He sat down. No one spoke. After a while, someone coughed."
production.hint: "The prose is stripped of everything that is not action — and this stripping enacts... The absence of interiority is... The cough performs... The sentence rhythms create... What the passage conspicuously withholds is... The effect is..."

production.prompt: Identify and analyse the tone of a short piece of writing you choose — a political speech extract, an editorial, or a literary passage. Describe: the register, the implied attitude toward the subject and reader, the moments where the tone shifts, and what stylistic choices construct the tone.
production.hint: "The tone is [description] throughout, constructed primarily by [specific features: word choice, sentence length, hedging, rhetorical questions...]. The most revealing moment is [specific passage], where [analysis]. What this tone achieves is..."

title: C2 Synthesis — The Language as Instrument, Medium and System
description: The final lesson: what it means to have closed the cycle — to use English not merely as communication but as a precision instrument, an intellectual medium and a system you inhabit.

explanation.text: C2 is not the end of language learning — it is the point at which the language stops being the primary object of attention and becomes instead the medium through which attention moves. THREE METAPHORS FOR C2: INSTRUMENT: at C2, English is a precision instrument. Like a surgeon's scalpel, it can do exactly what is needed with minimal collateral damage. You reach for the word that does exactly the work required, not the word that approximately does it. MEDIUM: at C2, English becomes a medium — like the water a fish moves through — present everywhere but not requiring constant attention. Thought and language begin to coincide; the friction between them diminishes. SYSTEM: at C2, you have internalised a system — not a collection of rules but a network of patterns, registers, collocations and discourse conventions that generate appropriate language automatically. THE REAL DIFFERENCE C2 MAKES: it is not that the C2 user says more, or says longer things, or uses more complex grammar. It is that they say precisely what they mean, exactly when they mean it, in exactly the right form, and the language never becomes the obstacle. "The language stops limiting the thought." WHAT REMAINS: even at C2, there are moments of imprecision, gaps in cultural knowledge, and registers not yet fully internalised. The difference is that the C2 user knows where those gaps are, can compensate for them, and can continue to close them — not as a student, but as an autonomous practitioner of the language.
explanation.note: "The language stops limiting the thought." — this is the practical criterion of C2. At A1, the language cannot say what the mind means. At B1, it approximates. At C1, it reaches. At C2, it arrives — with enough precision that the gap between thought and expression is no longer an obstacle to communication, and is experienced instead as a creative challenge.
explanation.tip: The most important habit of a C2 user is continued reading of complex, varied English texts — not for study, but for pleasure and for contact with the range of the language. At C2, exposure to excellent writing continues to expand and refine the lexical, syntactic and stylistic range in ways that no explicit instruction can match. The language grows from use.

examples.en: At C2, the question shifts from 'Can I say this?' to 'What is the most precise way to say this — and why?'
The precision that C2 affords is not merely technical. It is the precision that allows thought itself to be held more clearly.
What separates C2 from C1 is not, ultimately, grammatical — it is the ability to shape language to thought rather than shape thought to the available language.
The gaps that remain at C2 are known gaps — the practitioner can name them, compensate for them and continue to close them autonomously.
A C2 user reading a text in English no longer reads it as a foreign language — they read it as a text, attending to meaning, style and implication rather than to the code.
The language, at this point, is no longer an obstacle. It is an instrument — and what matters now is what you choose to do with it.

listening.text: At C2: not 'Can I say this?' but 'What is the most precise way to say this — and why?'
The precision that C2 affords allows thought itself to be held more clearly.
C2 is the ability to shape language to thought — not thought to the available language.
The gaps that remain at C2 are known gaps — the practitioner can name them and close them autonomously.
A C2 reader no longer reads in a foreign language — they read a text, attending to meaning and style.
The language is no longer an obstacle. It is an instrument. What matters now is what you do with it.
The language stops limiting the thought. That is the criterion. When it is met, the cycle is closed.

repetition.text: Not 'Can I say this?' but 'What is the most precise way to say this — and why?'
C2: shaping language to thought, not thought to the available language.
The gaps that remain are known gaps — they can be named and closed autonomously.
The language is no longer an obstacle. It is an instrument. What matters now is what you do with it.

practice.prompt: "The language stops limiting the thought." This is:
options: the practical criterion of C2 — the point at which the gap between thought and expression is no longer an obstacle
a description of fluency at B1
practice.answer: the practical criterion of C2 — the point at which the gap between thought and expression is no longer an obstacle

practice.prompt: What distinguishes C2 from C1 in practical terms?

C2 is only for native speakers
practice.answer: C2 users can shape language to thought; C1 users sometimes shape thought to available language

"The language is no longer an [BLANK]. It is an instrument." (obstacle)

practice.answer: The language stops limiting the thought

production.prompt: Write a 200-word reflection on your own relationship with English at the level you have reached. Be analytically honest: where does the language feel like an instrument? Where does it still feel like an obstacle? What are your known gaps? What would closing them require? Write this in English, at the highest level you can achieve.
production.hint: This is not a grammar exercise — it is an act of self-knowledge in a language. The quality of the English is part of the answer.

production.prompt: Write a 200-word final synthesis of what you have understood about language learning at C1-C2 level — not a summary of content, but a genuine analytical claim about what mastery of a language actually involves and what it makes possible.
production.hint: "What mastery of a language ultimately involves is not... but rather... The distinction that matters is between... and... At the highest levels, the question shifts from... to... What the language makes possible, at C2, is..."

title: Contrastive Stress and Prosodic Strategy — I Didn't Say HE Stole It
description: The full system of contrastive stress as a meaning-making device: how shifting the nuclear stress transforms the communicative content of the same string of words.

explanation.text: CONTRASTIVE STRESS at C2 is not a pronunciation feature — it is a semantic and pragmatic device. The sentence "I didn't say he stole the money" contains six possible nuclear stress positions, each generating a different communicative act. THE SIX MEANINGS: 1. "I didn't say he stole the money." → someone else said it, not me. 2. "I didn't say he stole the money." → I denied it, or communicated it differently. 3. "I didn't say HE stole the money." → I said someone else stole it. 4. "I didn't say he STOLE the money." → I said he took it, borrowed it, or another verb. 5. "I didn't say he stole THE money." → I said he stole something else. 6. "I didn't say he stole the MONEY." → I said he stole something else. PROSODIC STRATEGY beyond individual sentences: Stress on new information (the normal default) vs stress on given information to create contrast: "We thought the RESULTS were wrong — turns out the METHODOLOGY was wrong." STRESS IN ACADEMIC PRESENTATION: stressing the key term to help the listener track the argument: "The data reveal a CORRELATION — not, I want to be clear, a CAUSAL relationship." STRESS AND IMPLICATION: stressing a word that the listener has not said, creating the implicature that the unstressed reading would be wrong: "The evidence SUGGESTS this — it does not prove it."
explanation.note: A C2 speaker uses contrastive stress instrumentally: to correct, to contrast, to clarify, to implicate and to emphasise. The C1 speaker uses it correctly; the C2 speaker uses it strategically — choosing where to place emphasis as a rhetorical act.
explanation.tip: "The evidence SUGGESTS this — it does not PROVE it." — the double stress (suggest + prove) performs two acts simultaneously: it hedges the claim and it pre-empts the objection. The listener hears both the evidence and the epistemological caution in the same breath. This is prosodic precision at C2.

sounds.name: Contrastive Stress — Shifting Nuclear Stress to Change Meaning
sounds.description: In English, the default nuclear stress falls on the final content word. Moving the stress away from this default creates contrast, correction or implication. The stressed word is what the speaker is treating as new, contrastive or emphatic.
sounds.mouth_tip: Contrastive stress requires a pronounced pitch peak on the stressed syllable — noticeably higher than surrounding syllables — combined with greater length and amplitude. The contrast should be audible: "he STOLE the money" (stole is new info) vs "HE stole the money" (the identity is what is being emphasised).

words.en: "I didn't say HE stole it." (someone else did)
"I didn't say he STOLE it." (took without permission vs borrowed)
"I didn't SAY he stole it." (implied it, wrote it, showed it)
"The data SUGGEST this — they don't PROVE it."
"We thought the RESULTS were wrong — turns out the METHODOLOGY was."
"That's certainly ONE way of reading it." (stress on "one" implicates: I prefer another)

sounds.name: Prosodic Strategy — Stress for Argument Structure
sounds.description: In academic presentation and debate, stress is used strategically to help the listener follow the argumentative structure — to know what is being claimed, what is being contrasted, what is being qualified and what is being emphasised.
sounds.mouth_tip: In extended argument, vary stress placement to mark argumentative transitions: a new term receives nuclear stress when introduced; given terms receive less stress unless being explicitly contrasted. The listener should be able to follow your argument structure through stress alone.

words.en: "The evidence SUGGESTS — not PROVES — a correlation."
"We are not arguing X. We are arguing Y." (stress on X and Y marks the contrast)
"The FIRST consideration... the SECOND... and the THIRD — the most important..."
"This is not merely a TECHNICAL problem. It is a POLITICAL one."
"The claim is MODEST. The implications are NOT."
"SOME of the data support this. Not ALL."

minimal_pairs.a: "I didn't say HE stole it." — stress on "he": someone else stole it
minimal_pairs.b: "I didn't say he STOLE it." — stress on "stole": he did something else with it
minimal_pairs.note: same words, same grammar, entirely different communicative act

minimal_pairs.a: "The evidence SUGGESTS this." — suggests, not proves: epistemic hedge via stress
minimal_pairs.b: "The evidence suggests THIS." — the evidence points to this conclusion, not another
minimal_pairs.note: stress position changes what is being qualified vs what is being identified

minimal_pairs.a: "SOME data support the claim." — not all: scalar implication via stress
minimal_pairs.b: "Some DATA support the claim." — the data, not other sources, is what supports it
minimal_pairs.note: stress on scalar term creates implicature; stress on noun identifies the source

minimal_pairs.a: "That's ONE way of reading it." — there are others: implicature via stress
minimal_pairs.b: "That's one WAY of reading it." — reading is possible but other interpretations might be more than just ways
minimal_pairs.note: "one" under stress implicates: I prefer a different way; stress on "way" keeps it open

minimal_pairs.a: "The proposal is AMBITIOUS." — ambitious, not realistic: implicature of overreach
minimal_pairs.b: "The proposal is ambitious." — neutral assessment, no contrastive stress
minimal_pairs.note: contrastive stress on adjective creates implicature of the unstressed alternative

repetition.text: "I didn't say HE stole it." / "I didn't say he STOLE it." — practise both. Same words; entirely different meaning.
"The evidence SUGGESTS this — it does not PROVE it." — double contrastive stress performing hedging and pre-emption simultaneously.
"This is not merely a TECHNICAL problem. It is a POLITICAL one." — stress marks the key contrast.
"SOME data support the claim. Not ALL." — scalar stress + explicit denial.

production.prompt: Read each sentence aloud six times, shifting the nuclear stress to each successive content word. Note how the meaning and implication change each time: "The committee did not approve the revised proposal." (shift stress through: committee / did not / approve / revised / proposal). Then write a sentence for each stress pattern that makes explicit what is being implied.
production.hint: Stress on "committee": another body approved it. Stress on "did not": contrast with what someone claimed happened. Stress on "approve": they did something else (noted, rejected, deferred). Stress on "revised": they approved the original. Stress on "proposal": they approved something else.

production.prompt: Prepare and deliver (write as spoken) a 150-word academic argument in which you use prosodic strategy explicitly — mark at least five places where stress placement is doing specific argumentative work: introducing a contrast, hedging a claim, pre-empting an objection, marking a progression, or signalling an implication.
production.hint: Write the stress marks in the text as capitals: "The data SUGGEST — not PROVE — a pattern. The FIRST thing to note is... The SECOND is more significant... What the evidence does NOT support is the move from X to Y."

title: Discourse Intonation — Irony, Concession, Suspense and Rhetorical Framing
description: The full repertoire of discourse intonation patterns: how pitch movement across extended speech signals irony, concession, suspense, list structure and rhetorical framing.

explanation.text: DISCOURSE INTONATION at C2 goes beyond individual sentence intonation into the patterning of pitch across stretches of speech. It is the prosodic architecture of discourse. INTONATION AND IRONY: ironic statements often carry a level or slightly falling tone where a rising or enthusiastic tone would be expected. "That went well." with falling monotone, after a disaster — the absence of the expected positive intonation signals irony. "Quite the success story." said flatly, with no rising pitch — the gap between the positive lexis and the flat delivery creates the ironic gap. INTONATION AND CONCESSION: the fall-rise (→↘↗) on the conceded point signals: "I acknowledge this is true but..." The subsequent assertion carries a full fall, signalling commitment: "The evidence is limited ↘↗ — but the direction is clear. ↘" INTONATION AND SUSPENSE: building to a key term by using a series of rising tones on the preliminary elements, then a decisive fall on the culminating point: "The cause was not technical → not managerial → not even cultural — it was structural. ↘" INTONATION AND LIST STRUCTURE: in a rhetorical list, the first two items carry rising tones (signalling continuation) and the final item carries a full fall (signalling completion): "not for financial reasons ↗, not for operational reasons ↗, but for political ones ↘." HIGH FALL vs LOW FALL: high fall (from high pitch to low) signals strong assertion, confidence, or finality. Low fall (from mid pitch to low) signals measured, understated, perhaps ironic assertion.
explanation.note: The FALL-RISE is the single most important intonation pattern for conveying nuanced meaning in academic and professional speech. It signals: "I am committed enough to say this — but I retain reservations." The fall-rise on "The evidence is consistent ↘↗" is very different from a full fall: the full fall commits; the fall-rise qualifies.
explanation.tip: "The rise-fall (↗↘) on a statement signals strong commitment plus emotional involvement — used for warmth, insistence or enthusiastic assertion. "That's a GREAT idea. ↗↘" / "I KNEW this would happen. ↗↘" It is the intonation of someone who genuinely means what they say and wants you to feel it. Contrast it with the fall-rise, which qualifies, and the full fall, which simply commits.

sounds.name: Ironic Intonation — The Gap Between Word and Tone
sounds.description: Irony in English speech is often marked by a conspicuous absence: the positive intonation that would be expected for the positive lexis is missing. The flat, level or falling tone applied to words that should rise creates the ironic gap.
sounds.mouth_tip: For spoken irony: flatten your intonation where it would naturally rise. Hold a level pitch on the stressed syllable rather than peaking. The listener reads the gap between the lexis and the expected prosody.

words.en: "That went well." — flat, after a disaster. Irony through tonal flatness.
"Quite the achievement." — level tone on "achievement" = ironic.
"A model of transparency." — monotone where enthusiasm would be expected.
"How very surprising." — flat, when said of something entirely predictable.
"What excellent timing." — level, when the timing is disastrously bad.
"I'm sure that's helpful." — flat or slightly falling: ironic scepticism.

sounds.name: Suspense Intonation — Building to the Key Term
sounds.description: A rhetorical series of rising tones on preliminary elements building to a decisive full fall on the culminating claim. Creates anticipation, directs attention to the final term and signals the speaker's confident resolution of the opening tension.
sounds.mouth_tip: Each preliminary element should carry a rising tone that keeps the listener expecting more. The final term receives a full, decisive fall — from high pitch to low. The contrast between the rises and the final fall creates the rhetorical impact.

words.en: "Not technical → not managerial → not cultural ↗ — structural. ↘"
"Not what was said ↗, not when it was said ↗ — but WHY. ↘"
"Not the cost ↗, not the timeline ↗, not the resources ↗ — the will. ↘"
"I have considered the objections ↗, weighed the evidence ↗, listened to the critics ↗ — and I remain convinced. ↘"
"The first consideration → the second → the third — and the most important. ↘"
"It was not a failure of knowledge ↗. Not a failure of resources ↗. It was a failure of will. ↘"

minimal_pairs.a: "The evidence is consistent." ↘ (full fall — committed assertion)
minimal_pairs.b: "The evidence is consistent." ↘↗ (fall-rise — assertion with reservation)
minimal_pairs.note: full fall commits; fall-rise qualifies — same words, different epistemic stance signalled entirely by intonation

minimal_pairs.b: "That's interesting." ↘↗ (fall-rise — polite but reserved: I'm not convinced)
minimal_pairs.note: the fall-rise turns a compliment into a qualified response

minimal_pairs.a: "That went well." ↗ (genuinely positive, sincere — rising tone)
minimal_pairs.b: "That went well." ↘ (flat, level, monotone — ironic: it went badly)
minimal_pairs.note: the irony is created by using a falling or level tone where the positive meaning would predict a rise

minimal_pairs.b: "A great idea." ↘ (full fall — measured, confident endorsement)
minimal_pairs.note: rise-fall adds emotional warmth and enthusiasm; full fall is cooler, more authoritative

minimal_pairs.a: List items with rising tones ↗ ↗ ↗ — signals continuation, not yet done
minimal_pairs.b: Final list item with full fall ↘ — signals completion and commitment
minimal_pairs.note: the shift from rises to final fall is the intonational marker of list completion

repetition.text: "That went well." — with genuine rising tone (sincere). Then with flat/falling monotone (ironic). The difference is the tonal gap, not the words.
"Not technical ↗, not managerial ↗, not cultural ↗ — structural. ↘" — practise the build and the fall.
"The evidence is consistent ↘↗" — fall-rise for reservation. "The evidence is consistent. ↘" — full fall for commitment.
"A great idea ↗↘" — rise-fall for warmth. "A great idea. ↘" — full fall for authority. Both are positive; only the emotional colour differs.

production.prompt: Practise producing the following sentences with two different intonation patterns and note what changes: (1) "The proposal has merit." — full fall vs fall-rise. (2) "That's an interesting approach." — genuine rising vs flat/ironic. (3) "The evidence is clear." — high fall (strong commitment) vs low fall (measured understatement). For each, write one sentence that captures the implied meaning of each version.
production.hint: (1) Full fall: "The proposal has merit — I support it." Fall-rise: "The proposal has merit — though serious questions remain." (2) Rising: sincere, engaged. Flat: "one way of looking at it" implication. (3) High fall: assertive, confident. Low fall: measured, possibly hedging.

production.prompt: Record (write as spoken with intonation marks) a 150-word rhetorical closing statement that uses: (a) one ironic intonation pattern, (b) one suspense build with culminating fall, (c) one fall-rise for concession followed by a full fall for the rebuttal. Mark all intonation patterns explicitly.
production.hint: "The evidence, I should note, is [ironic monotone]: overwhelming. ↘ The cause of this failure was not [↗ not] technical [↗], not managerial [↗] — it was systemic. ↘ The situation is, in many respects, not ideal [↘↗] — but the direction is clear ↘, and the course of action is not."

title: What's the Weather Like? — Tempo e Clima
description: Falar sobre o tempo é uma das conversas mais comuns em inglês. Aprenda a descrever e perguntar sobre o clima.

explanation.text: Para falar sobre o tempo em inglês, usamos principalmente o verbo IT IS (It's): IT'S + adjetivo: It's hot. / It's cold. / It's sunny. / It's cloudy. / It's windy. / It's foggy. / IT'S + gerúndio para chuva e neve: It's raining. / It's snowing. / PARA PERGUNTAR: What's the weather like today? (Como está o tempo hoje?) / What's the weather like in London? / How's the weather? (mais informal). TEMPERATURA: It's 30 degrees. / It's very hot today. / It's freezing! (está congelando — informal para muito frio). EXPRESSÕES ÚTEIS: It's a beautiful day! / The weather is terrible today. / It's getting colder. / Take an umbrella — it might rain.
explanation.note: Em inglês, o sujeito IT é obrigatório nas frases sobre tempo — nunca dizemos "Is raining" ou "Is hot". O IT não tem tradução direta; é apenas uma regra gramatical do inglês: "It's raining" = "Está chovendo".
explanation.tip: A pergunta "What's the weather like?" é muito mais natural do que "How is the weather?" no inglês cotidiano. Use-a sempre que quiser perguntar sobre o tempo.

examples.en: It's sunny and warm today — a perfect day for a walk.
examples.pt: Está ensolarado e quente hoje — um dia perfeito para caminhar.

examples.en: What's the weather like in Rio de Janeiro in January?
examples.pt: Como está o tempo no Rio de Janeiro em janeiro?

examples.en: It's raining heavily. Don't forget your umbrella.
examples.pt: Está chovendo muito. Não esqueça o seu guarda-chuva.

examples.en: It's very cold today — it's almost zero degrees.
examples.pt: Está muito frio hoje — está quase zero graus.

examples.en: Is it snowing in Canada right now?
examples.pt: Está nevando no Canadá agora?

examples.en: The weather is cloudy and windy — I think it's going to rain.
examples.pt: O tempo está nublado e ventoso — acho que vai chover.

listening.text: What's the weather like today?
listening.translation: Como está o tempo hoje?

listening.text: It's sunny and warm.
listening.translation: Está ensolarado e quente.

listening.text: It's raining. Take your umbrella.
listening.translation: Está chovendo. Leve o seu guarda-chuva.

listening.text: Is it cold in London?
listening.translation: Está frio em Londres?

listening.text: Yes, it's very cold — it's only five degrees.
listening.translation: Sim, está muito frio — só cinco graus.

listening.text: It's a beautiful day today!
listening.translation: É um dia lindo hoje!

listening.text: The weather is terrible. It's cloudy and windy.
listening.translation: O tempo está horrível. Está nublado e ventoso.

repetition.text: It's sunny today.
repetition.translation: Está ensolarado hoje.

repetition.text: It's raining.
repetition.translation: Está chovendo.

repetition.text: What's the weather like?
repetition.translation: Como está o tempo?

repetition.text: It's very cold — take your jacket.
repetition.translation: Está muito frio — leve seu casaco.

practice.prompt: Como se pergunta "Como está o tempo hoje?"
Qual é a tradução de "It's raining heavily"?
[BLANK] sunny and warm today. (It's)
What's the weather [BLANK] in São Paulo? (like)
Monte a frase:
practice.answer: It's very cold — only ten degrees

production.prompt: Descreva o tempo onde você está agora. Use pelo menos 3 frases com: What's the weather like today? / It's... / It's (verb)ing.
production.hint: "It's sunny and warm today. It's about 28 degrees. It's not raining, but it's a little cloudy."

production.prompt: Escreva 4 frases descrevendo o tempo em 4 cidades diferentes que você conhece.
production.hint: "In London, it's cold and cloudy. In Dubai, it's very hot and dry. In Oslo, it's snowing in winter. In Rio de Janeiro, it's sunny and humid."

title: The Four Seasons — As Quatro Estações
description: As estações do ano em inglês: vocabulário, características e como falar sobre o que você faz em cada estação.

explanation.text: AS QUATRO ESTAÇÕES: spring = primavera / summer = verão / autumn (British) / fall (American) = outono / winter = inverno. COMO USAR: In spring, it's warm and flowers bloom. / In summer, it's hot and sunny. / In autumn, the leaves change colour. / In winter, it's cold and it often snows. PERGUNTAS: What's your favourite season? / What do you do in summer? / What's the weather like in autumn? MESES DE CADA ESTAÇÃO (hemisfério norte): Spring: March, April, May / Summer: June, July, August / Autumn: September, October, November / Winter: December, January, February. IMPORTANTE: No Brasil e no hemisfério sul, as estações são invertidas — quando é verão no Brasil, é inverno na Europa.
explanation.note: Em inglês americano, "fall" é mais comum do que "autumn" para outono. Em inglês britânico, usa-se quase sempre "autumn". Ambas são corretas — escolha uma e use consistentemente.
explanation.tip: "In + season" (without "the"): "In summer" — correto. "In the summer" também é possível e muito comum na fala natural. Ambas as formas são aceitáveis.

examples.en: My favourite season is summer — I love the beach.
examples.pt: Minha estação favorita é o verão — adoro a praia.

examples.en: In spring, the weather is mild and there are flowers everywhere.
examples.pt: Na primavera, o tempo é ameno e há flores em todo lugar.

examples.en: In winter, it gets dark very early in the evening.
examples.pt: No inverno, fica escuro muito cedo à noite.

examples.en: What do you usually do in autumn?
examples.pt: O que você costuma fazer no outono?

examples.en: I love winter because I enjoy skiing and hot chocolate.
examples.pt: Adoro o inverno porque gosto de esquiar e de chocolate quente.

examples.en: In Brazil, December is summer — it's the opposite of Europe.
examples.pt: No Brasil, dezembro é verão — é o oposto da Europa.

listening.text: What's your favourite season?
listening.translation: Qual é a sua estação favorita?

listening.text: My favourite season is spring.
listening.translation: Minha estação favorita é a primavera.

listening.text: In summer, it's hot and sunny.
listening.translation: No verão, está quente e ensolarado.

listening.text: What do you do in winter?
listening.translation: O que você faz no inverno?

listening.text: I stay at home and watch films.
listening.translation: Fico em casa e assisto a filmes.

listening.text: Autumn is beautiful — the leaves are red and yellow.
listening.translation: O outono é lindo — as folhas são vermelhas e amarelas.

listening.text: In Brazil, the seasons are the opposite of Europe.
listening.translation: No Brasil, as estações são o oposto da Europa.

repetition.text: My favourite season is summer.
repetition.translation: Minha estação favorita é o verão.

repetition.text: In winter, it's cold and dark.
repetition.translation: No inverno, está frio e escuro.

repetition.text: What do you do in spring?
repetition.translation: O que você faz na primavera?

repetition.text: I love autumn — the colours are beautiful.
repetition.translation: Adoro o outono — as cores são lindas.

practice.prompt: Qual é a tradução de "autumn"?
Quais meses correspondem ao inverno no hemisfério norte?
My favourite season [BLANK] winter. (is)
[BLANK] spring, the weather is warm and mild. (In)
Monte a frase:

production.prompt: Escreva 4 frases — uma sobre cada estação — descrevendo o tempo e o que você gosta de fazer.
production.hint: "In summer, it's hot and I love swimming. In autumn, it's cool and I enjoy long walks. In winter, it's cold and I stay at home. In spring, it's warm and I go to the park."

production.prompt: Responda: What's your favourite season and why? Escreva 3 a 4 frases.
production.hint: "My favourite season is summer because I love the warm weather and the long days. In summer, I go to the beach and spend time with friends. The days are sunny and I feel happy."

title: Clothes and Colours — Roupas e Cores
description: Vocabulário essencial de roupas e cores: o que vestir, descrever o que alguém está usando e perguntar sobre roupas.

explanation.text: ROUPAS (CLOTHES / CLOTHING): shirt = camisa / T-shirt = camiseta / dress = vestido / trousers / pants = calça / skirt = saia / jacket = jaqueta / coat = casaco / shoes = sapatos / boots = botas / socks = meias / hat = chapéu / bag = bolsa. CORES: red = vermelho / blue = azul / green = verde / yellow = amarelo / black = preto / white = branco / orange = laranja / pink = rosa / brown = marrom / grey = cinza / purple = roxo. COMO USAR: WEARING — She is wearing a red dress. / He is wearing blue trousers and a white shirt. / I am not wearing a jacket today. PERGUNTAR: What are you wearing? / What colour is your bag? ADJETIVOS vêm ANTES do substantivo: a red shirt (não: a shirt red). Cor + roupa: a black jacket / blue jeans / white shoes.
explanation.note: Em inglês britânico: "trousers". Em inglês americano: "pants". Nos dois dialetos, "jeans" é plural (my jeans are blue — não: my jeans is).
explanation.tip: "I'm wearing" usa o Present Continuous porque é uma ação em andamento no momento. "I wear" (Present Simple) descreve um hábito: "I always wear black." / "Today, I'm wearing a blue shirt."

examples.en: She is wearing a beautiful red dress and black shoes.
examples.pt: Ela está usando um vestido vermelho lindo e sapatos pretos.

examples.en: What colour is your jacket? — It's dark blue.
examples.pt: De que cor é sua jaqueta? — É azul escuro.

examples.en: He always wears a white shirt to work.
examples.pt: Ele sempre usa camisa branca no trabalho.

examples.en: I need a new coat — it's getting cold.
examples.pt: Eu preciso de um casaco novo — está esfriando.

examples.en: I'm not wearing my glasses today.
examples.pt: Não estou usando meus óculos hoje.

examples.en: Those brown boots are very nice — where did you get them?
examples.pt: Essas botas marrons são muito bonitas — onde você as comprou?

listening.text: What are you wearing today?
listening.translation: O que você está usando hoje?

listening.text: I'm wearing a blue T-shirt and black jeans.
listening.translation: Estou usando uma camiseta azul e calça jeans preta.

listening.text: She is wearing a green dress.
listening.translation: Ela está usando um vestido verde.

listening.text: What colour is your bag?
listening.translation: De que cor é sua bolsa?

listening.text: It's brown.
listening.translation: É marrom.

listening.text: He always wears a white shirt to work.
listening.translation: Ele sempre usa camisa branca no trabalho.

listening.text: I need new shoes — these are too old.
listening.translation: Preciso de sapatos novos — esses estão muito velhos.

repetition.text: I'm wearing a blue shirt and grey trousers.

repetition.text: What colour is your jacket?
repetition.translation: De que cor é sua jaqueta?

repetition.text: She is wearing a red dress.

practice.prompt: Como se diz "Ela está usando uma camisa verde" em inglês?
